questions.question: Explain the structure of a node in a singly linked list. What are its components?
keyPoints: Node has two parts: DATA FIELD and LINK/POINTER FIELD
Data field stores actual information
Link field stores address of next node
Last node's link points to NULL
Self-referential structure in C

questions.question: What are the advantages of singly linked lists over arrays?
keyPoints: Dynamic size - grows/shrinks at runtime
Efficient insertion/deletion at beginning O(1)
No memory wastage - only allocate what's needed
No pre-allocation of memory required
Flexible memory allocation from heap

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a node at the beginning of a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Create a new node using malloc
Assign data to new node
Set new node's next pointer to current head
Update head pointer to point to new node
Time complexity is O(1)

questions.question: What are the disadvantages of singly linked lists?
keyPoints: No random access - must traverse from head
Higher memory per element due to storing pointers
No backward traversal possible
Poor cache locality (non-contiguous memory)
Cannot directly access nth element efficiently

questions.question: Explain the process of deleting a node from a singly linked list.
keyPoints: Case 1: Delete first node - update head to second node
Case 2: Delete middle/end - traverse to node before target
Update previous node's next to skip target node
Free the memory of deleted node
Time complexity: O(1) for deletion after given node, O(n) for finding it

questions.question: How do you find the middle element of a singly linked list in one traversal?
keyPoints: Use two pointers: slow and fast
Move slow by one step, fast by two steps
When fast reaches end, slow is at middle
For odd length: exact middle
For even length: second of two middle elements
Time complexity: O(n)

questions.question: What is a dummy head node? Why is it used?
keyPoints: Dummy head is a placeholder node at the beginning
It does not contain meaningful data
Simplifies insertion and deletion at the beginning
Eliminates special case handling for first node
Makes code cleaner and reduces edge case bugs

questions.question: Write a C program to implement a singly linked list with functions to: (a) Create a node (b) Insert at beginning (c) Insert at end (d) Delete a node (e) Display the list.
keyPoints: Define struct Node with data and next pointer
Implement createNode function with malloc
InsertAtBeginning: new->next = head, head = new
InsertAtEnd: traverse to last node, last->next = new
DeleteNode: handle head deletion and middle deletion
Display: traverse and print each node's data
Handle edge cases: empty list, single node

questions.question: Explain the algorithm to reverse a singly linked list. Discuss both iterative and recursive approaches with their time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Iterative: Use three pointers (prev, curr, next)
Initialize prev = NULL, curr = head
While curr != NULL: save next, reverse link, move prev and curr
Finally set head = prev
Iterative Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
Recursive: Reverse the rest, fix head node
Recursion uses call stack
Recursive Time: O(n), Space: O(n)
Comparison: Iterative preferred for space efficiency

questions.question: How do you detect a cycle in a singly linked list? Explain Floyd's cycle detection algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Use two pointers moving at different speeds
Slow pointer moves 1 step, fast pointer moves 2 steps
If cycle exists, fast will eventually meet slow
Initialize both at head, iterate until fast reaches NULL or they meet
Why it works: fast catches up by 1 node per iteration in cycle
Time complexity: O(n), Space complexity: O(1)
Example: In a cycle of 5 nodes, they will definitely meet
Can also find starting node of cycle using mathematical analysis

questions.question: Compare singly linked lists and arrays. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Memory: Arrays contiguous, linked lists scattered
Size: Arrays fixed, linked lists dynamic
Access: Arrays O(1), linked lists O(n)
Insertion at beginning: Arrays O(n), linked lists O(1)
Insertion at end: Arrays O(1), linked lists O(n) for singly
Cache: Arrays better due to locality
Memory overhead: Arrays less, linked lists store pointers
Prefer arrays when: random access needed, size known, cache performance important
Prefer linked lists when: frequent insertions/deletions, size unknown, no random access needed
